<result>Now I have enough information to compile a comprehensive overview. Here is the full research report:

---

# Comprehensive Guide to Scraping Clothing & Shopping Data (2025-2026)

## 1. Scraping Approaches Compared

### A. SERP APIs (Google Shopping Scraping)

**How it works:** You query a SERP API with a search term (e.g., "men's leather jacket") and it returns structured Google Shopping results including product titles, prices, images, sellers, ratings, and links.

**Key providers and pricing:**

| Provider | Starting Price | Notes |
|----------|---------------|-------|
| **SerpApi** | $25/mo (1K searches) to $275/mo (30K searches) | Dedicated Google Shopping endpoint; returns position, title, price, old price, source, thumbnail, rating, reviews, delivery info, extensions |
| **Bright Data SERP API** | ~$1/1K requests | Also includes proxy infrastructure |
| **Oxylabs Web Scraper API** | $0.50/1K results | Includes Google Shopping support |
| **Scrapfly** | $30/mo (200K credits) to $500/mo (5.5M credits) | General-purpose but supports Shopping queries; extra credits needed for JS rendering (+5) and residential proxies (+25) |

**Pros:**
- Aggregates results across many retailers in one query
- Structured JSON output, no parsing needed
- Handles Google's anti-bot measures for you
- Good for price comparison and competitive monitoring

**Cons:**
- Limited to what Google Shopping indexes (not all products)
- Data depth is shallow -- you get listing-level data, not full product pages
- No access to stock levels, full size runs, materials, or detailed descriptions
- Cost scales linearly with query volume

**Best for:** Price comparison, competitive monitoring across many retailers, trend discovery.

---

### B. Direct Retailer Scraping

**How it works:** You build or use scrapers that visit individual retailer websites (Zara, H&M, ASOS, Nordstrom, etc.) and extract product data from their HTML or internal APIs.

**Approaches:**
- **HTTP-based scraping** (requests + BeautifulSoup/lxml): Works for server-rendered pages. Fast, lightweight, low cost.
- **Internal/undocumented APIs**: Many retailers load product data via XHR/fetch requests to internal JSON APIs. Reverse-engineering these yields clean, structured data without HTML parsing. ASOS, H&M, and Zara all use internal APIs heavily.
- **Browser automation** (Playwright/Puppeteer): Required for heavily JS-rendered sites. More resource-intensive.

**Retailer-specific notes:**

| Retailer | Difficulty | Notes |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| **ASOS** | Medium | Has internal APIs; product catalog is large (80K+ items); uses Akamai bot protection |
| **Zara** | Medium-Hard | Heavy SPA architecture; internal APIs exist but rotate; Cloudflare protection |
| **H&M** | Medium | Internal APIs available; moderate bot protection |
| **Nordstrom** | Hard | Strong anti-bot (Akamai/PerimeterX); requires residential proxies |
| **Nike** | Hard | Heavy anti-bot protection; frequently changes page structure |
| **Uniqlo** | Medium | Relatively straightforward internal APIs |
| **Shein** | Hard | Aggressive anti-bot; frequently changes structure |

**Pros:**
- Richest data: full descriptions, all images, size/color matrices, materials, reviews, stock status
- Can capture entire catalogs
- Can monitor price changes and stock over time
- No per-query cost (beyond infrastructure)

**Cons:**
- High maintenance burden -- sites change frequently
- Must handle anti-bot measures (CAPTCHAs, fingerprinting, rate limiting)
- Legal grey area (see Section 3)
- Each retailer requires a separate scraper
- Some sites are extremely difficult to scrape reliably

**Best for:** Deep product data, catalog monitoring, building a comprehensive fashion database.

---

### C. Marketplace APIs (Official)

**Amazon Product Advertising API (PA-API 5.0):**
- **Status:** Free to use but **being deprecated April 30, 2026** -- migrating to "Creators API"
- **Requirements:** Must be an Amazon Associate (affiliate)
- **Data available:** Item info, images, offers/pricing, browse nodes, variations, search refinements
- **Limitations:** Rate-limited based on your revenue generation; must generate sales to maintain access; cannot store price data long-term per TOS
- **Best for:** Affiliate sites that need real-time Amazon pricing

**eBay APIs:**
- **Browse API:** Product search, item details, images, prices, seller info
- **Finding API:** Advanced search with filters
- **Free tier** available with application key
- **Rate limits:** Vary by API call type (typically 5,000 calls/day on basic tier)
- **Data:** Product title, price, images, item specifics (size, color, brand), condition, seller info, shipping

**Shopify Storefront API:**
- Many DTC (direct-to-consumer) fashion brands run on Shopify
- Public Storefront API available if the merchant enables it
- Can access product data, variants, images, prices, availability

**Pros:**
- Legal and sanctioned
- Structured, reliable data
- No anti-bot concerns
- Free or low cost

**Cons:**
- Amazon PA-API being deprecated in 2026
- Limited to products on that marketplace
- Rate limits can be restrictive
- TOS restrictions on data storage and usage
- You must comply with affiliate/attribution requirements

---

### D. Fashion-Specific Data Providers (Enterprise)

These are enterprise-grade platforms that have already built the scraping infrastructure and provide analyzed, structured fashion data:

| Provider | Focus | Key Features | Pricing Model |
|----------|-------|--------------|---------------|
| **EDITED** | Retail intelligence | Trend analysis, competitive benchmarking, assortment optimization, pricing strategy. Covers thousands of retailers. Offices in London, Dallas, Sofia. 51-200 employees. | Enterprise only; request demo. Estimated $30K-$100K+/year |
| **Trendalytics** | AI trend forecasting | Predictive search/social data, TikTok intelligence, market movers, social media metrics, product development support | Enterprise; no public pricing. Estimated $20K-$60K+/year |
| **Heuritech** | Visual AI trend prediction | Analyzes millions of social images monthly; detects ~2,000 fashion attributes (prints, colors, fabrics, shapes); forecasts 24 months ahead; claims 91%+ accuracy | SaaS platform or API; no public pricing |
| **Retviews (Lectra)** | Competitive analysis | Real-time pricing intelligence, assortment planning, competitor alerts, product matching. 98.5% data accuracy claimed. 90% client adoption in 1 month. Used by Bally, Diesel, Etam | Enterprise; no public pricing |
| **DataWeave** | Retail analytics | Pricing intelligence, digital shelf analytics, assortment analytics, content analytics. 99%+ product matching accuracy via LLMs + computer vision. Clients include Costco, Home Depot | Enterprise; no public pricing |
| **Coresight Research** | Retail research/data | Proprietary datasets, store metrics, consumer surveys, market size data. Covers apparel, luxury, beauty, grocery | Subscription tiers; advisory retainers |

**Pros:**
- No scraping infrastructure to build or maintain
- Pre-cleaned, structured, analyzed data
- Trend forecasting and competitive intelligence built in
- Legal -- they handle data licensing
- Domain expertise and support

**Cons:**
- Very expensive (typically $20K-$100K+ per year)
- Designed for enterprise brands/retailers, not startups
- Data access may be limited to their platform (no raw data export in some cases)
- May not cover niche or emerging brands

---

### E. Browser Automation (Puppeteer / Playwright)

**How it works:** A headless or headed browser (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit) is programmatically controlled to navigate sites, wait for JS rendering, and extract data from the fully rendered DOM.

**Tools:**
- **Playwright** (Microsoft): Supports Chromium, Firefox, WebKit. Python, Node.js, Java, .NET. Currently the preferred choice for new projects.
- **Puppeteer** (Google): Chromium/Chrome only. Node.js. Mature ecosystem.
- **Selenium:** Older, slower, but widest language support. Less recommended for new projects.
- **Crawlee** (Apify): Open-source framework built on Playwright/Puppeteer with built-in anti-detection, proxy rotation, session management, and autoscaling.

**When required:**
- Single-page applications (React/Vue/Angular stores like Zara, Nike)
- Sites that load products via infinite scroll
- Sites requiring user interactions (clicking size selectors, color swatches)
- Sites with heavy client-side rendering

**Anti-detection add-ons:**
- `playwright-stealth` / `puppeteer-extra-plugin-stealth`: Patches browser fingerprints
- Custom user-agent rotation
- Realistic mouse movements and scrolling
- Cookie and session management

**Pros:**
- Can handle any website regardless of JS complexity
- Sees exactly what a human browser sees
- Can interact with elements (dropdowns, filters, pagination)

**Cons:**
- Resource-intensive (each browser instance uses ~100-300MB RAM)
- Slower than HTTP-based scraping (5-10x)
- Higher proxy costs due to bandwidth
- Still detectable by advanced anti-bot systems
- Complex to scale

---

## 2. Data Available from Clothing Retailers

A comprehensive scraping setup can typically extract these data points:

### Core Product Data
| Data Point | Availability | Notes |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Product name/title | Universal | Always available |
| Brand | Universal | Sometimes part of title |
| Price (current) | Universal | May require locale/currency handling |
| Sale/discount price | Common | Often shown as strikethrough + new price |
| Original/MSRP price | Common | Not always distinguished from regular price |
| Product description | Universal | HTML formatted; varies in length |
| SKU/Product ID | Universal | Internal identifiers |
| Product URL | Universal | Canonical URL |

### Visual Data
| Data Point | Availability | Notes |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Primary image | Universal | Usually high-resolution |
| Gallery images | Very common | 3-8 images typical; may include model shots |
| Swatch images | Common | Small color/pattern thumbnails |
| Video content | Rare | Growing trend but still uncommon |
| 360-degree views | Rare | Mostly luxury retailers |

### Variant Data
| Data Point | Availability | Notes |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Available colors | Very common | May be names ("Midnight Blue") or hex codes |
| Available sizes | Very common | Format varies widely (S/M/L, 0-16, 28-42, etc.) |
| Size-specific stock | Common | Some sites show "Only 2 left" or "Out of stock" per size |
| Color-specific images | Common | Each color variant typically has its own image set |
| Color-specific prices | Occasional | Some colors may be priced differently |

### Material & Specifications
| Data Point | Availability | Notes |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Material/fabric composition | Common | "98% Cotton, 2% Elastane" -- often in description |
| Care instructions | Common | Wash/dry/iron details |
| Fit type | Common | "Slim Fit", "Regular", "Oversized" |
| Measurements | Occasional | Size charts with cm/inch measurements |
| Weight | Rare | More common for outerwear |
| Country of origin | Occasional | Increasingly required by regulations |

### Reviews & Social Proof
| Data Point | Availability | Notes |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Average rating | Common | Usually 1-5 stars |
| Review count | Common | Total number of reviews |
| Individual reviews | Common | Text, rating, date, reviewer name |
| Review images | Growing | Customer-uploaded photos |
| Fit feedback | Growing | "Runs small/large" crowdsourced data |
| Verified purchase flag | Common | Distinguishes verified buyers |

### Taxonomy & Organization
| Data Point | Availability | Notes |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Category hierarchy | Universal | e.g., Women > Clothing > Dresses > Midi Dresses |
| Sub-category | Universal | Usually 2-4 levels deep |
| Tags/attributes | Common | "Sustainable", "New Arrival", "Bestseller" |
| Collection/season | Occasional | "Spring 2026", "Resort Collection" |
| Related/similar products | Common | Cross-sell recommendations |

### Availability & Logistics
| Data Point | Availability | Notes |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| In-stock status | Universal | Binary or quantity-based |
| Delivery estimates | Common | "2-5 business days" |
| Store availability | Occasional | For omnichannel retailers |
| Return policy | Common | Usually site-wide, not per-product |

---

## 3. Legal Considerations

### Key Legal Frameworks

**Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA) -- United States:**
- The landmark **hiQ Labs v. LinkedIn** (2022) decision established that scraping publicly available data does not violate the CFAA. The Supreme Court declined to hear LinkedIn's appeal.
- However, this applies to *publicly accessible* data. Scraping behind a login wall (requiring credentials) could still violate the CFAA.
- The CFAA prohibits "unauthorized access" to computer systems. Courts have increasingly interpreted this narrowly for public websites.

**Terms of Service (ToS):**
- Most retailers explicitly prohibit scraping in their ToS
- Violating ToS is generally a breach of contract, not a criminal matter
- However, ToS violations can lead to cease-and-desist letters, IP bans, and civil lawsuits
- The enforceability of "browsewrap" ToS (where you don't explicitly agree) is debatable

**robots.txt:**
- A voluntary standard; not legally binding in the US
- Ignoring robots.txt can be used as evidence of bad faith in legal proceedings
- Best practice: always respect robots.txt directives
- Some sites use robots.txt aggressively to block all bots

**GDPR (EU) and Privacy Laws:**
- Scraping personal data (reviewer names, user profiles) in the EU triggers GDPR compliance requirements
- Product data (prices, descriptions) is generally not personal data
- California's CCPA has similar protections for personal information

**Copyright:**
- Product images are copyrighted by the retailer/brand
- Product descriptions may be copyrighted
- Factual data (prices, sizes, colors) is generally not copyrightable
- Using scraped images in your own app/site without permission is risky

### Practical Legal Guidance

1. **Safest:** Use official APIs (Amazon PA-API, eBay API) or licensed data providers (EDITED, Bright Data datasets)
2. **Moderate risk:** Scrape publicly available factual data (prices, availability) while respecting robots.txt and rate limits
3. **Higher risk:** Scraping and republishing copyrighted content (images, descriptions), scraping behind login walls, ignoring cease-and-desist requests
4. **Recommendations:**
   - Consult a lawyer if scraping is core to your business
   - Keep scraping frequency reasonable (don't DDoS the site)
   - Respect robots.txt
   - Don't circumvent technical access controls
   - Don't scrape personal data without a legal basis
   - Attribute data sources where appropriate
   - Be prepared to stop scraping a site if you receive a C&D

---

## 4. Technical Challenges

### Anti-Bot Measures by Difficulty

**Tier 1 -- Basic (easy to bypass):**
- User-agent checking
- IP-based rate limiting
- Basic referrer checking
- Simple cookie requirements

**Tier 2 -- Intermediate:**
- **Cloudflare** (used by Zara, many DTC brands): JavaScript challenges, browser fingerprinting, turnstile CAPTCHAs
- **Akamai Bot Manager** (used by Nordstrom, Nike, many large retailers): Device fingerprinting, behavioral analysis, session validation
- **DataDome** (growing adoption): ML-based bot detection, real-time behavioral analysis
- Rate limiting with sliding windows
- Dynamic CSS class names (changes with each deploy)

**Tier 3 -- Advanced (difficult):**
- **PerimeterX (now HUMAN)**: Advanced fingerprinting, proof-of-work challenges
- Canvas fingerprinting
- WebGL fingerprinting
- TLS fingerprinting (JA3/JA4)
- Behavioral analysis (mouse movements, scroll patterns, typing cadence)
- Honeypot links/forms
- Dynamic rendering that serves different content to bots

### Content Loading Challenges

- **Infinite scroll:** Products load as you scroll; requires browser automation or API reverse-engineering
- **Lazy-loaded images:** Images only load when scrolled into view
- **Dynamic filtering:** Size/color availability loaded via AJAX after initial page load
- **Client-side rendering:** Entire product pages rendered in JavaScript
- **A/B testing:** Different users see different page layouts
- **Geo-blocking:** Different products/prices by region; requires regional proxies

### Data Normalization Challenges

- **Size formats:** S/M/L vs 0-16 vs 28-42 vs UK 8/10/12 vs EU 36/38/40
- **Color names:** "Midnight" vs "Navy" vs "Dark Blue" -- same color, different names
- **Material descriptions:** Inconsistent formatting across retailers
- **Price formats:** Different currencies, tax inclusion varies by country
- **Category taxonomies:** Every retailer has a different hierarchy

---

## 5. Cost Comparison

### Approach 1: Build Your Own Scraper

| Cost Component | Monthly Estimate | Notes |
|---------------|-----------------|-------|
| Developer time (build) | $5K-$20K one-time | Depends on number of retailers, complexity |
| Developer time (maintain) | $2K-$5K/mo ongoing | Sites change frequently; expect breakage |
| Proxy service | $100-$2,000/mo | Residential proxies: $1.50-$4/GB. Expect 50-500 GB/mo depending on scale |
| Compute (cloud hosting) | $50-$500/mo | Browser automation requires more compute |
| CAPTCHA solving | $50-$500/mo | If needed; services like 2Captcha at $2-3/1K solves |
| Storage | $20-$100/mo | Database + image storage |
| **Total (small scale, 5-10 retailers)** | **$300-$1,500/mo + dev time** | |
| **Total (medium scale, 20-50 retailers)** | **$1,000-$5,000/mo + dev time** | |

**Proxy pricing benchmarks (current):**

| Provider | Residential Price/GB | Notes |
|----------|---------------------|-------|
| **Decodo (formerly Smartproxy)** | $1.50-$3.50/GB | 115M+ IPs, 195+ locations. Regular plans at $1.50-$2.00/GB. Pay-as-you-go at $3.50/GB |
| **Bright Data** | $2.50-$5/GB (50% off promotional) | Largest pool (72M+ IPs) |
| **Oxylabs** | $4-$8/GB | 100M+ IPs |

### Approach 2: SERP APIs / Scraping APIs

| Service | Monthly Cost | What You Get |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| **SerpApi** | $25-$275/mo | 1K-30K Google Shopping searches |
| **Oxylabs Web Scraper** | $50-$500/mo | Structured data from supported sites |
| **Bright Data Web Unlocker** | Starts at $1/1K requests | Handles anti-bot for you |
| **Scrapfly** | $30-$500/mo | 200K-5.5M API credits |
| **Diffbot** | $0-$899/mo | 10K-1M credits; automatic data extraction |

### Approach 3: Pre-Built Datasets

| Provider | Cost | What You Get |
|----------|------|-------------|
| **Bright Data Fashion Datasets** | Starting $250/100K records | 77 fashion datasets, 5B+ total records. Sources include Zara, H&M, ASOS, luxury brands, Amazon. Full product data. Delivery via JSON/CSV/Parquet to S3/GCS/Snowflake |
| **Bright Data Retail Insights** | Starting $250/mo | Analyzed retail intelligence |
| **Bright Data Managed Acquisition** | Starting $1,500/mo | Custom data collection with their team |

### Approach 4: Enterprise Data Providers

| Provider | Estimated Annual Cost | What You Get |
|----------|----------------------|-------------|
| **EDITED** | $30K-$100K+/year | Full retail intelligence platform |
| **Trendalytics** | $20K-$60K+/year | AI trend forecasting + market intelligence |
| **Retviews (Lectra)** | $25K-$80K+/year | Competitive analysis, real-time pricing |
| **Heuritech** | $30K-$100K+/year | Visual AI trend prediction |
| **DataWeave** | $30K-$100K+/year | Pricing, assortment, digital shelf analytics |

### Cost Comparison Summary

| Approach | Setup Cost | Monthly Cost | Data Depth | Maintenance |
|----------|-----------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| DIY scraping | High ($5-20K) | Low-Med ($300-5K) | Deepest | Very High |
| SERP/Scraping APIs | Low | Med ($25-900) | Shallow-Med | Low |
| Pre-built datasets | None | Med ($250-1.5K) | Deep | None |
| Enterprise providers | None | High ($2-8K+) | Deep + analyzed | None |

---

## 6. Recommended Tech Stack

### For Building Your Own Scraper

**Core Language: Python**

| Component | Recommended Tools | Alternatives |
|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| **HTTP requests** | `httpx` (async), `requests` | `aiohttp`, `urllib3` |
| **HTML parsing** | `selectolax` (fastest), `BeautifulSoup4` | `lxml`, `parsel` |
| **Browser automation** | `Playwright` (Python) | `Selenium`, `Puppeteer` (Node.js) |
| **Scraping framework** | `Scrapy` (large scale), `Crawlee` (anti-detection) | `colly` (Go) |
| **Anti-detection** | `playwright-stealth`, `curl_cffi` (TLS fingerprint matching) | `undetected-chromedriver` |
| **Proxy management** | Decodo/Smartproxy, Bright Data, Oxylabs | NetNut, IPRoyal |
| **CAPTCHA solving** | 2Captcha, Anti-Captcha, CapSolver | Manual solving (low scale) |
| **Scheduling** | `Celery` + `Redis`, `APScheduler` | `Airflow`, `Prefect`, cron |
| **Data storage** | `PostgreSQL` (structured), `MongoDB` (flexible) | `ClickHouse` (analytics), `S3` (images/raw) |
| **Data validation** | `Pydantic` | `marshmallow`, `cerberus` |
| **Image storage** | AWS S3, Cloudflare R2 | Google Cloud Storage, MinIO |
| **Monitoring** | `Sentry` (errors), `Grafana` (metrics) | `Datadog`, custom alerts |

### Architecture Recommendation

```
[Scheduler (Celery/Airflow)]
        |
        v
[Task Queue (Redis/RabbitMQ)]
        |
        v
[Scraper Workers (Python + Playwright/httpx)]
        |
        v
[Proxy Rotation Layer (Smartproxy/Bright Data)]
        |
        v
[Data Pipeline (Pydantic validation -> PostgreSQL)]
        |
        v
[Image Pipeline (Download -> S3/R2)]
        |
        v
[API Layer (FastAPI) -> Your Application]
```

### For Using Managed Services

If you want to avoid building infrastructure, this stack is simpler:

| Component | Recommended |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Data ingestion** | Bright Data Datasets API or Scraping API |

# Comprehensive Guide to Scraping Clothing & Shopping Data (2025-2026)

## 1. Scraping Approaches Compared

### A. SERP APIs (Google Shopping Scraping)

**How it works:** You query a SERP API with a search term (e.g., "men's leather jacket") and it returns structured Google Shopping results including product titles, prices, images, sellers, ratings, and links.

**Key providers and pricing:**

| Provider | Starting Price | Notes |
|----------|---------------|-------|
| **SerpApi** | $25/mo (1K searches) to $275/mo (30K searches) | Dedicated Google Shopping endpoint; returns position, title, price, old price, source, thumbnail, rating, reviews, delivery info, extensions |
| **Bright Data SERP API** | ~$1/1K requests | Also includes proxy infrastructure |
| **Oxylabs Web Scraper API** | $0.50/1K results | Includes Google Shopping support |
| **Scrapfly** | $30/mo (200K credits) to $500/mo (5.5M credits) | General-purpose but supports Shopping queries; extra credits needed for JS rendering (+5) and residential proxies (+25) |

**Pros:**
- Aggregates results across many retailers in one query
- Structured JSON output, no parsing needed
- Handles Google's anti-bot measures for you
- Good for price comparison and competitive monitoring

**Cons:**
- Limited to what Google Shopping indexes (not all products)
- Data depth is shallow -- you get listing-level data, not full product pages
- No access to stock levels, full size runs, materials, or detailed descriptions
- Cost scales linearly with query volume

**Best for:** Price comparison, competitive monitoring across many retailers, trend discovery.

---

### B. Direct Retailer Scraping

**How it works:** You build or use scrapers that visit individual retailer websites (Zara, H&M, ASOS, Nordstrom, etc.) and extract product data from their HTML or internal APIs.

**Approaches:**
- **HTTP-based scraping** (requests + BeautifulSoup/lxml): Works for server-rendered pages. Fast, lightweight, low cost.
- **Internal/undocumented APIs**: Many retailers load product data via XHR/fetch requests to internal JSON APIs. Reverse-engineering these yields clean, structured data without HTML parsing. ASOS, H&M, and Zara all use internal APIs heavily.
- **Browser automation** (Playwright/Puppeteer): Required for heavily JS-rendered sites. More resource-intensive.

**Retailer-specific notes:**

| Retailer | Difficulty | Notes |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| **ASOS** | Medium | Has internal APIs; product catalog is large (80K+ items); uses Akamai bot protection |
| **Zara** | Medium-Hard | Heavy SPA architecture; internal APIs exist but rotate; Cloudflare protection |
| **H&M** | Medium | Internal APIs available; moderate bot protection |
| **Nordstrom** | Hard | Strong anti-bot (Akamai/PerimeterX); requires residential proxies |
| **Nike** | Hard | Heavy anti-bot protection; frequently changes page structure |
| **Uniqlo** | Medium | Relatively straightforward internal APIs |
| **Shein** | Hard | Aggressive anti-bot; frequently changes structure |

**Pros:**
- Richest data: full descriptions, all images, size/color matrices, materials, reviews, stock status
- Can capture entire catalogs
- Can monitor price changes and stock over time
- No per-query cost (beyond infrastructure)

**Cons:**
- High maintenance burden -- sites change frequently
- Must handle anti-bot measures (CAPTCHAs, fingerprinting, rate limiting)
- Legal grey area (see Section 3)
- Each retailer requires a separate scraper
- Some sites are extremely difficult to scrape reliably

**Best for:** Deep product data, catalog monitoring, building a comprehensive fashion database.

---

### C. Marketplace APIs (Official)

**Amazon Product Advertising API (PA-API 5.0):**
- **Status:** Free to use but **being deprecated April 30, 2026** -- migrating to "Creators API"
- **Requirements:** Must be an Amazon Associate (affiliate)
- **Data available:** Item info, images, offers/pricing, browse nodes, variations, search refinements
- **Limitations:** Rate-limited based on your revenue generation; must generate sales to maintain access; cannot store price data long-term per TOS
- **Best for:** Affiliate sites that need real-time Amazon pricing

**eBay APIs:**
- **Browse API:** Product search, item details, images, prices, seller info
- **Finding API:** Advanced search with filters
- **Free tier** available with application key
- **Rate limits:** Vary by API call type (typically 5,000 calls/day on basic tier)
- **Data:** Product title, price, images, item specifics (size, color, brand), condition, seller info, shipping

**Shopify Storefront API:**
- Many DTC (direct-to-consumer) fashion brands run on Shopify
- Public Storefront API available if the merchant enables it
- Can access product data, variants, images, prices, availability

**Pros:**
- Legal and sanctioned
- Structured, reliable data
- No anti-bot concerns
- Free or low cost

**Cons:**
- Amazon PA-API being deprecated in 2026
- Limited to products on that marketplace
- Rate limits can be restrictive
- TOS restrictions on data storage and usage
- You must comply with affiliate/attribution requirements

---

### D. Fashion-Specific Data Providers (Enterprise)

These are enterprise-grade platforms that have already built the scraping infrastructure and provide analyzed, structured fashion data:

| Provider | Focus | Key Features | Pricing Model |
|----------|-------|--------------|---------------|
| **EDITED** | Retail intelligence | Trend analysis, competitive benchmarking, assortment optimization, pricing strategy. Covers thousands of retailers. Offices in London, Dallas, Sofia. 51-200 employees. | Enterprise only; request demo. Estimated $30K-$100K+/year |
| **Trendalytics** | AI trend forecasting | Predictive search/social data, TikTok intelligence, market movers, social media metrics, product development support | Enterprise; no public pricing. Estimated $20K-$60K+/year |
| **Heuritech** | Visual AI trend prediction | Analyzes millions of social images monthly; detects ~2,000 fashion attributes (prints, colors, fabrics, shapes); forecasts 24 months ahead; claims 91%+ accuracy | SaaS platform or API; no public pricing |
| **Retviews (Lectra)** | Competitive analysis | Real-time pricing intelligence, assortment planning, competitor alerts, product matching. 98.5% data accuracy claimed. 90% client adoption in 1 month. Used by Bally, Diesel, Etam | Enterprise; no public pricing |
| **DataWeave** | Retail analytics | Pricing intelligence, digital shelf analytics, assortment analytics, content analytics. 99%+ product matching accuracy via LLMs + computer vision. Clients include Costco, Home Depot | Enterprise; no public pricing |
| **Coresight Research** | Retail research/data | Proprietary datasets, store metrics, consumer surveys, market size data. Covers apparel, luxury, beauty, grocery | Subscription tiers; advisory retainers |

**Pros:**
- No scraping infrastructure to build or maintain
- Pre-cleaned, structured, analyzed data
- Trend forecasting and competitive intelligence built in
- Legal -- they handle data licensing
- Domain expertise and support

**Cons:**
- Very expensive (typically $20K-$100K+ per year)
- Designed for enterprise brands/retailers, not startups
- Data access may be limited to their platform (no raw data export in some cases)
- May not cover niche or emerging brands

---

### E. Browser Automation (Puppeteer / Playwright)

**How it works:** A headless or headed browser (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit) is programmatically controlled to navigate sites, wait for JS rendering, and extract data from the fully rendered DOM.

**Tools:**
- **Playwright** (Microsoft): Supports Chromium, Firefox, WebKit. Python, Node.js, Java, .NET. Currently the preferred choice for new projects.
- **Puppeteer** (Google): Chromium/Chrome only. Node.js. Mature ecosystem.
- **Selenium:** Older, slower, but widest language support. Less recommended for new projects.
- **Crawlee** (Apify): Open-source framework built on Playwright/Puppeteer with built-in anti-detection, proxy rotation, session management, and autoscaling.

**When required:**
- Single-page applications (React/Vue/Angular stores like Zara, Nike)
- Sites that load products via infinite scroll
- Sites requiring user interactions (clicking size selectors, color swatches)
- Sites with heavy client-side rendering

**Anti-detection add-ons:**
- `playwright-stealth` / `puppeteer-extra-plugin-stealth`: Patches browser fingerprints
- Custom user-agent rotation
- Realistic mouse movements and scrolling
- Cookie and session management

**Pros:**
- Can handle any website regardless of JS complexity
- Sees exactly what a human browser sees
- Can interact with elements (dropdowns, filters, pagination)

**Cons:**
- Resource-intensive (each browser instance uses ~100-300MB RAM)
- Slower than HTTP-based scraping (5-10x)
- Higher proxy costs due to bandwidth
- Still detectable by advanced anti-bot systems
- Complex to scale

---

## 3. Legal Considerations

### Key Legal Frameworks

**Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA) -- United States:**
- The landmark **hiQ Labs v. LinkedIn** (2022) decision established that scraping publicly available data does not violate the CFAA. The Supreme Court declined to hear LinkedIn's appeal.
- However, this applies to *publicly accessible* data. Scraping behind a login wall (requiring credentials) could still violate the CFAA.
- The CFAA prohibits "unauthorized access" to computer systems. Courts have increasingly interpreted this narrowly for public websites.

**Terms of Service (ToS):**
- Most retailers explicitly prohibit scraping in their ToS
- Violating ToS is generally a breach of contract, not a criminal matter
- However, ToS violations can lead to cease-and-desist letters, IP bans, and civil lawsuits
- The enforceability of "browsewrap" ToS (where you don't explicitly agree) is debatable

**robots.txt:**
- A voluntary standard; not legally binding in the US
- Ignoring robots.txt can be used as evidence of bad faith in legal proceedings
- Best practice: always respect robots.txt directives
- Some sites use robots.txt aggressively to block all bots

**GDPR (EU) and Privacy Laws:**
- Scraping personal data (reviewer names, user profiles) in the EU triggers GDPR compliance requirements
- Product data (prices, descriptions) is generally not personal data
- California's CCPA has similar protections for personal information

**Copyright:**
- Product images are copyrighted by the retailer/brand
- Product descriptions may be copyrighted
- Factual data (prices, sizes, colors) is generally not copyrightable
- Using scraped images in your own app/site without permission is risky

### Practical Legal Guidance

1. **Safest:** Use official APIs (Amazon PA-API, eBay API) or licensed data providers (EDITED, Bright Data datasets)
2. **Moderate risk:** Scrape publicly available factual data (prices, availability) while respecting robots.txt and rate limits
3. **Higher risk:** Scraping and republishing copyrighted content (images, descriptions), scraping behind login walls, ignoring cease-and-desist requests
4. **Recommendations:**
   - Consult a lawyer if scraping is core to your business
   - Keep scraping frequency reasonable (don't DDoS the site)
   - Respect robots.txt
   - Don't circumvent technical access controls
   - Don't scrape personal data without a legal basis
   - Attribute data sources where appropriate
   - Be prepared to stop scraping a site if you receive a C&D

---

## 4. Technical Challenges

### Anti-Bot Measures by Difficulty

**Tier 1 -- Basic (easy to bypass):**
- User-agent checking
- IP-based rate limiting
- Basic referrer checking
- Simple cookie requirements

**Tier 2 -- Intermediate:**
- **Cloudflare** (used by Zara, many DTC brands): JavaScript challenges, browser fingerprinting, turnstile CAPTCHAs
- **Akamai Bot Manager** (used by Nordstrom, Nike, many large retailers): Device fingerprinting, behavioral analysis, session validation
- **DataDome** (growing adoption): ML-based bot detection, real-time behavioral analysis
- Rate limiting with sliding windows
- Dynamic CSS class names (changes with each deploy)

**Tier 3 -- Advanced (difficult):**
- **PerimeterX (now HUMAN)**: Advanced fingerprinting, proof-of-work challenges
- Canvas fingerprinting
- WebGL fingerprinting
- TLS fingerprinting (JA3/JA4)
- Behavioral analysis (mouse movements, scroll patterns, typing cadence)
- Honeypot links/forms
- Dynamic rendering that serves different content to bots

### Content Loading Challenges

- **Infinite scroll:** Products load as you scroll; requires browser automation or API reverse-engineering
- **Lazy-loaded images:** Images only load when scrolled into view
- **Dynamic filtering:** Size/color availability loaded via AJAX after initial page load
- **Client-side rendering:** Entire product pages rendered in JavaScript
- **A/B testing:** Different users see different page layouts
- **Geo-blocking:** Different products/prices by region; requires regional proxies

### Data Normalization Challenges

- **Size formats:** S/M/L vs 0-16 vs 28-42 vs UK 8/10/12 vs EU 36/38/40
- **Color names:** "Midnight" vs "Navy" vs "Dark Blue" -- same color, different names
- **Material descriptions:** Inconsistent formatting across retailers
- **Price formats:** Different currencies, tax inclusion varies by country
- **Category taxonomies:** Every retailer has a different hierarchy

---

## 6. Recommended Tech Stack

| **Enterprise competitive intelligence** | EDITED or Retviews | $30K-$100K/year |
| **Trend forecasting** | Trendalytics or Heuritech | $20K-$100K/year |
| **One-time data collection** | Bright Data managed acquisition or Apify actors | $1,500+ one-time |
| **Real-time stock monitoring** | DIY scraper with scheduling | $500-$2,000/mo + significant dev time |

The most practical path for most startups and small teams is to start with **Bright Data's pre-built fashion datasets** for historical/bulk data ($250/mo starting) combined with a **targeted DIY scraper** for a handful of priority retailers where you need real-time or very specific data. Enterprise platforms like EDITED and Trendalytics are only justified if you are a fashion brand or retailer making buying/pricing decisions at scale.</result>

# Comprehensive SERP API Research Report

## 1. What is a SERP API?

A **SERP API** (Search Engine Results Page API) is a service that programmatically queries search engines (Google, Bing, Yahoo, etc.) and returns structured data (typically JSON) from the results pages. Instead of building and maintaining your own web scraping infrastructure -- dealing with proxies, CAPTCHAs, IP rotation, and anti-bot measures -- a SERP API handles all of that and delivers clean, parsed data.

**Core use cases:**
- SEO monitoring and rank tracking
- Competitive intelligence
- Price monitoring and e-commerce product research
- Market research and trend analysis
- Ad intelligence (tracking paid placements)
- Academic research

---

## 2. Provider-by-Provider Breakdown

### A. SerpApi (serpapi.com) -- The Market Leader

**Supported Search Engines:** Google, Bing, Yahoo, DuckDuckGo, Yandex, Baidu, Naver. Also supports marketplace platforms: Amazon, eBay, Walmart, Home Depot, Tripadvisor, Yelp, YouTube, Apple App Store.

**Google Search Types:** Web, Images, Shopping, News, Videos, Local/Maps, Patents, Knowledge Graph, Direct Answers, Recipes, Events, Finance, Flights, Hotels, Jobs, Trending Topics.

**Pricing Tiers:**

| Plan | Monthly Cost | Searches/Month | Cost per Search | Throughput |
|------|-------------|----------------|-----------------|------------|
| **Free** | $0 | 250 | $0.00 | 50/hour |
| Starter | $25 | 1,000 | $0.025 | 200/hour |
| Developer | $75 | 5,000 | $0.015 | 1,000/hour |
| Production | $150 | 15,000 | $0.010 | 3,000/hour |
| Big Data | $275 | 30,000 | ~$0.009 | 6,000/hour |
| Enterprise | Custom | Custom | Custom | Custom |

**Key Details:**
- Only successful searches count toward quota
- Full refund within 7 days if <20% searches used
- "U.S. Legal Shield" included on all plans
- "ZeroTrace Mode" on Free and Enterprise tiers
- Response time: ~1.67 seconds (fastest in market)
- 99.988% uptime reported
- JSON output only

**Strengths:** Widest search engine coverage, fastest response times, excellent documentation, mature product, strong structured data parsing.

**Weaknesses:** Most expensive per-search at lower tiers, no HTML output option, small free tier (250 searches).

---

### B. Serper.dev -- The Budget Speed Champion

**Supported Search Engines:** Google only

**Search Types:** Web, Images, News, Maps/Places, Videos, Shopping, Scholar, Patents, Autocomplete

**Pricing:**
- **Free tier:** 2,500 queries (no credit card required)
- Paid pricing is not publicly listed on their marketing pages; they advertise themselves as "the world's fastest and cheapest Google Search API"
- Based on known industry data: approximately **$1-2 per 1,000 queries** at paid tiers, making it one of the cheapest options
- Response time: 1-2 seconds

**Strengths:** Extremely affordable, generous free tier (2,500 queries), very fast, simple API, great for prototyping.

**Weaknesses:** Google-only (no Bing, Yahoo, etc.), less comprehensive data parsing than SerpApi, pricing not transparent on website.

---

### C. SearchAPI.io (searchapi.io) -- The Value Middle Ground

**Supported Search Engines:** Google (with Google Shopping, Google Scholar, Google News, etc.)

**Pricing Tiers:**

| Plan | Monthly Cost | Searches/Month | Cost per 1K Searches |
|------|-------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Free** | $0 | 100 | -- |
| Developer | $40 | 10,000 | $4.00 |
| Production | $100 | 35,000 | $3.00 (approx) |
| BigData | $250 | 100,000 | $2.50 |
| Scale | $500 | 250,000 | $2.00 |
| Octo 500K | $900 | 500,000 | $1.80 |
| Octo 1M | $1,500 | 1,000,000 | $1.50 |
| Octo 2M | $2,800 | 2,000,000 | $1.40 |
| Octo 5M | $5,000 | 5,000,000 | $1.00 |

**Key Details:**
- Only successful (200 status) requests are billed
- Rate limit: max 20% of monthly credits per hour
- 99.9% SLA on Developer+ plans
- Legal protection guarantee (up to $2M coverage) on Production+
- `zero_retention` parameter for compliance
- Geo-targeting and premium proxies included

**Strengths:** Excellent price scaling for high volume, strong Google Shopping support, good documentation, legal protection.

**Weaknesses:** Smaller free tier (100 searches), primarily Google-focused, newer/less established than SerpApi.

---

### D. Bright Data SERP API -- The Enterprise Powerhouse

**Supported Search Engines:** Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo, Yandex, Baidu, Yahoo, Naver (7 engines across 195 countries)

**Search Types:** Web, Shopping, Maps, Hotels, Images, Trends, Reviews, News, Flights, Videos, Jobs, Google Ads

**Pricing:**

| Tier | Monthly Cost | Cost per 1K Results |
|------|-------------|-------------------|
| Pay-as-you-go | Variable | $1.50 |
| 380K results/mo | $499 | $1.30 |
| 900K results/mo | $999 | $1.10 |
| 2M results/mo | $1,999 | $1.00 |
| Enterprise | Custom | Custom |

**Key Details:**
- Matching bonus: up to $500 on first deposit of $500+
- Output formats: JSON, HTML, and Markdown
- No concurrent request limits
- Async mode with 99.99% success rate (48-hour collection window)
- City-level geo-targeting across 195 countries
- Pay only for successful delivery
- Response time: under 5 seconds typical, as fast as 1 second

**Strengths:** Massive infrastructure, 7 search engines, 195 countries, no concurrency limits, async mode, Markdown output option.

**Weaknesses:** High minimum for volume plans ($499/mo), lower success rate (98.42%) compared to competitors reporting 100%, higher per-result cost at low volumes.

---

### E. Oxylabs SERP Scraper API -- The Enterprise Competitor

**Pricing:**
- Starts from **$49/month**
- **$0.50 per 1,000 results** (at the low end, this is the cheapest per-result for parsed data)
- Oxylabs blog comparison states: $1.35 per 1K results at their SERP tier
- 100% success rate reported
- Response time: ~6.04 seconds

**Supported Engines:** Google (including Shopping), Bing, Baidu, Yandex

**Key Details:**
- AI-powered "OxyCopilot" assistant for building scrapers
- Google Shopping search supported
- Structured JSON output
- Extensive proxy infrastructure

**Strengths:** Very competitive pricing at scale, high success rate, enterprise-grade infrastructure, AI assistant for scraper creation.

**Weaknesses:** Slower response times (~6 seconds), enterprise-oriented (less friendly for small projects), pricing tiers not fully transparent.

---

### F. Rayobyte -- The Budget Option

**Pricing:**
- **$0.0018 per scrape** ($1.80 per 1K)
- **5,000 free scrapes** to start
- No monthly commitment required

**Performance:** 100% success rate, ~6.53 second response time

**Strengths:** No monthly commitment, very affordable, good free allowance.

**Weaknesses:** Basic features, slower response times, less sophisticated data parsing.

---

### G. ScaleSerp / ValueSerp (now Traject Data)

These have been acquired by **Traject Data**. ScaleSerp was historically popular for its generous free tier (formerly 250 free searches/month on ValueSerp). Current pricing is hard to pin down as their websites have been migrated. Historically:

- **ScaleSerp:** ~$29/month for 1,000 searches, scaling to $999/month for 100,000 searches
- **ValueSerp:** Positioned as the "best value" option with similar tiered pricing

---

## 3. Pricing Comparison Summary

| Provider | Free Tier | Cheapest Paid | Cost per 1K (Low Vol) | Cost per 1K (High Vol) | Response Time |
|----------|-----------|--------------|----------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **SerpApi** | 250/mo | $25/mo (1K) | $25.00 | ~$9.17 | ~1.7s |
| **Serper.dev** | 2,500 queries | ~$1-2/1K est. | ~$1-2 | ~$1 | 1-2s |
| **SearchAPI.io** | 100/signup | $40/mo (10K) | $4.00 | $1.00 | Moderate |
| **Bright Data** | Trial | $499/mo (380K) | $1.50 (PAYG) | $1.00 | <5s |
| **Oxylabs** | Trial | $49/mo | ~$1.35 | ~$0.50 | ~6s |
| **Rayobyte** | 5,000 | PAYG | $1.80 | $1.80 | ~6.5s |

---

## 4. Rate Limits Summary

| Provider | Free Tier Rate | Paid Rate Limits |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **SerpApi** | 50/hour | 200-6,000/hour (by plan) |
| **Serper.dev** | Not published | Not published |
| **SearchAPI.io** | Limited | Max 20% of monthly credits/hour |
| **Bright Data** | -- | No concurrent request limits |
| **Oxylabs** | -- | Enterprise-scale, not published |

---

## 5. Shopping-Specific Capabilities

This is critical for your use case. Here is what each provider offers for Google Shopping:

### SerpApi Google Shopping API
**Endpoint:** `/search?engine=google_shopping`

**Shopping search parameters:**
- `min_price` / `max_price` -- price range filters
- `sort_by` -- price low-to-high or high-to-low
- `free_shipping` -- filter for free shipping only
- `on_sale` -- filter for sale items
- `small_business` -- filter for small business products

**Data fields returned per shopping result:**
- `title` -- product name
- `product_id` -- unique product identifier
- `product_link` -- link to product page
- `price` / `extracted_price` -- current price (string and numeric)
- `old_price` / `extracted_old_price` -- original price when on sale
- `source` -- retailer name (e.g., "Nordstrom", "ASOS")
- `source_icon` -- retailer logo
- `rating` -- star rating (e.g., 4.4)
- `reviews` -- review count
- `snippet` -- review summary text
- `thumbnail` / `thumbnails` -- product images
- `tag` -- sale badges (e.g., "51% OFF")
- `delivery` -- shipping info
- `extensions` -- extra metadata
- `immersive_product_page_token` -- token for detailed product drill-down

**Immersive Product API (drill-down):**
- Full product `title`, `brand`, `price_range`
- `variants` -- **size, color, capacity options** (critical for clothing)
- Per-variant `available` stock status
- Multiple seller comparison: `price`, `original_price`, `discount`, `shipping`, `tax`, `total`
- `payment_methods` per seller
- `critic_ratings` and `user_reviews` with images
- `videos` (YouTube reviews)
- `discussions_and_forums` (Reddit mentions)
- `related_searches`

### SearchAPI.io Google Shopping
**Endpoint:** `engine=google_shopping`

**Parameters:** `price_min`, `price_max`, `is_on_sale`, `is_free_delivery`, `is_small_business`, `sort_by` (price_low_to_high, price_high_to_low, rating_high_to_low), `condition` (new/used)

**Data returned:** Shopping ads, product listings, popular products, interactive filters, pricing, ratings, reviews, delivery info, product tokens for detailed lookups. Related sub-APIs: Google Product, Google Product Offers, Google Product Reviews, Google Shopping Filters.

### Bright Data Google Shopping
Listed as a dedicated search type. Supports Shopping results across 195 countries. JSON, HTML, and Markdown output. Specific field-level documentation was not accessible but follows standard Google Shopping data structure.

### Serper.dev Shopping
Has a dedicated Shopping endpoint. Returns standard shopping result fields. Less detailed documentation publicly available.

---

## 6. Clothing/Fashion Data Availability

For clothing and fashion product scraping specifically, here is what matters and how each provider handles it:

### What You Need for Fashion Data:
1. **Size variants** -- S, M, L, XL, numerical sizes, etc.
2. **Color variants** -- with color swatches/images per color
3. **Price tracking** -- current price, original price, discount percentage
4. **Product images** -- multiple angles, on-model shots
5. **Brand information** -- brand name, brand page
6. **Material/composition** -- fabric details
7. **Ratings and reviews** -- customer feedback, fit information
8. **Merchant availability** -- which stores carry the item and at what price
9. **Sale/promotion tags** -- clearance, seasonal sales

### Provider Assessment for Fashion:

**SerpApi (Best for Fashion):**
- The **Immersive Product API** explicitly returns `variants` including size and color options with availability status
- Returns multi-seller price comparisons (critical for fashion price monitoring)
- `old_price` / `extracted_old_price` for tracking markdowns
- `tag` field captures sale badges ("51% OFF")
- Multiple thumbnail images
- Also supports **Amazon**, **Walmart**, and **eBay** product APIs, allowing cross-marketplace fashion price comparison
- **Limitation:** Google Shopping shows what Google indexes; not all fashion retailers participate in Google Shopping feeds equally

**SearchAPI.io (Strong Alternative):**
- Dedicated Google Product, Product Offers, and Product Reviews sub-APIs
- Condition filtering (new/used -- relevant for resale fashion)
- Rating-based sorting
- Product tokens for drill-down into individual items

**Bright Data (Best for Scale):**
- If you need to scrape thousands of fashion product pages across 195 countries for international pricing, Bright Data's infrastructure is unmatched
- Async mode means you can submit bulk queries and collect results within 48 hours
- Markdown output can be useful for processing product descriptions

**Serper.dev (Best for Budget Prototyping):**
- If you are building a fashion price comparison tool and want to prototype cheaply, the 2,500 free queries and ~$1-2/1K pricing is hard to beat
- Shopping endpoint available
- Less detailed variant/size data compared to SerpApi's Immersive Product API

### Key Limitation for Fashion Data:
**No SERP API can replace direct retailer scraping for complete fashion data.** Google Shopping results are aggregated from merchant product feeds, which means:
- Not all size/color variants may be indexed
- Real-time stock levels are approximate
- Detailed material/fabric composition is rarely in Google Shopping data
- Fit information (true to size, runs small) is not captured
- Many direct-to-consumer fashion brands (e.g., Everlane, Reformation) have limited Google Shopping presence

For comprehensive fashion data, you would likely need a **hybrid approach**: SERP API for discovery and price monitoring + direct scraping of individual retailer sites for detailed product attributes.

---

## 7. Recommendations by Use Case

| Use Case | Best Provider | Why |
|----------|--------------|-----|
| **Budget prototyping** | Serper.dev | 2,500 free queries, cheapest per search |
| **Best data quality** | SerpApi | Immersive Product API with variants, fastest response |
| **High volume (100K+/mo)** | SearchAPI.io | Best price scaling ($1/1K at 5M tier) |
| **Enterprise / global** | Bright Data or Oxylabs | 195 countries, async, no concurrency limits |
| **Fashion price monitoring** | SerpApi + SearchAPI.io | Best variant/size data + affordable scaling |
| **Multi-engine search** | SerpApi or Bright Data | Most search engine coverage |

---

## 8. Hidden Fees and Gotchas

- **SerpApi:** Only successful searches are billed (good). But at $25/1K searches on the Starter plan, costs add up fast for high-volume use.
- **SearchAPI.io:** The 20%-of-monthly-credits-per-hour rate limit can be restrictive if you need burst capacity.
- **Bright Data:** The $499/mo minimum for volume pricing is a significant commitment. Pay-as-you-go at $1.50/1K is reasonable but still has a learning curve with their async system.
- **Oxylabs:** Enterprise-oriented; expect sales calls and potentially custom contracts.
- **All providers:** Searches that return no results may or may not count toward your quota depending on the provider -- check each provider's policy.
- **Google Shopping specifics:** Some providers charge a shopping search the same as a regular search; others may charge differently. With SerpApi, a Google Shopping search costs one search credit, same as any other search.</result>

**Immersive Product API (drill-down):**
- Full product `title`, `brand`, `price_range`
- `variants` -- **size, color, capacity options** (critical for clothing)
- Per-variant `available` stock status
- Multiple seller comparison: `price`, `original_price`, `discount`, `shipping`, `tax`, `total`
- `payment_methods` per seller
- `critic_ratings` and `user_reviews` with images
- `videos` (YouTube reviews)
- `discussions_and_forums` (Reddit mentions)
- `related_searches`

### SearchAPI.io Google Shopping
**Endpoint:** `engine=google_shopping`

**Parameters:** `price_min`, `price_max`, `is_on_sale`, `is_free_delivery`, `is_small_business`, `sort_by` (price_low_to_high, price_high_to_low, rating_high_to_low), `condition` (new/used)

**Data returned:** Shopping ads, product listings, popular products, interactive filters, pricing, ratings, reviews, delivery info, product tokens for detailed lookups. Related sub-APIs: Google Product, Google Product Offers, Google Product Reviews, Google Shopping Filters.

### Bright Data Google Shopping
Listed as a dedicated search type. Supports Shopping results across 195 countries. JSON, HTML, and Markdown output. Specific field-level documentation was not accessible but follows standard Google Shopping data structure.

### Serper.dev Shopping
Has a dedicated Shopping endpoint. Returns standard shopping result fields. Less detailed documentation publicly available.

---

## 6. Clothing/Fashion Data Availability

For clothing and fashion product scraping specifically, here is what matters and how each provider handles it:

### What You Need for Fashion Data:
1. **Size variants** -- S, M, L, XL, numerical sizes, etc.
2. **Color variants** -- with color swatches/images per color
3. **Price tracking** -- current price, original price, discount percentage
4. **Product images** -- multiple angles, on-model shots
5. **Brand information** -- brand name, brand page
6. **Material/composition** -- fabric details
7. **Ratings and reviews** -- customer feedback, fit information
8. **Merchant availability** -- which stores carry the item and at what price
9. **Sale/promotion tags** -- clearance, seasonal sales

### Provider Assessment for Fashion:

**SerpApi (Best for Fashion):**
- The **Immersive Product API** explicitly returns `variants` including size and color options with availability status
- Returns multi-seller price comparisons (critical for fashion price monitoring)
- `old_price` / `extracted_old_price` for tracking markdowns
- `tag` field captures sale badges ("51% OFF")
- Multiple thumbnail images
- Also supports **Amazon**, **Walmart**, and **eBay** product APIs, allowing cross-marketplace fashion price comparison
- **Limitation:** Google Shopping shows what Google indexes; not all fashion retailers participate in Google Shopping feeds equally

**SearchAPI.io (Strong Alternative):**
- Dedicated Google Product, Product Offers, and Product Reviews sub-APIs
- Condition filtering (new/used -- relevant for resale fashion)
- Rating-based sorting
- Product tokens for drill-down into individual items

**Bright Data (Best for Scale):**
- If you need to scrape thousands of fashion product pages across 195 countries for international pricing, Bright Data's infrastructure is unmatched
- Async mode means you can submit bulk queries and collect results within 48 hours
- Markdown output can be useful for processing product descriptions

**Serper.dev (Best for Budget Prototyping):**
- If you are building a fashion price comparison tool and want to prototype cheaply, the 2,500 free queries and ~$1-2/1K pricing is hard to beat
- Shopping endpoint available
- Less detailed variant/size data compared to SerpApi's Immersive Product API

### Key Limitation for Fashion Data:
**No SERP API can replace direct retailer scraping for complete fashion data.** Google Shopping results are aggregated from merchant product feeds, which means:
- Not all size/color variants may be indexed
- Real-time stock levels are approximate
- Detailed material/fabric composition is rarely in Google Shopping data
- Fit information (true to size, runs small) is not captured
- Many direct-to-consumer fashion brands (e.g., Everlane, Reformation) have limited Google Shopping presence

For comprehensive fashion data, you would likely need a **hybrid approach**: SERP API for discovery and price monitoring + direct scraping of individual retailer sites for detailed product attributes.

---

## 7. Recommendations by Use Case

| Use Case | Best Provider | Why |
|----------|--------------|-----|
| **Budget prototyping** | Serper.dev | 2,500 free queries, cheapest per search |
| **Best data quality** | SerpApi | Immersive Product API with variants, fastest response |
| **High volume (100K+/mo)** | SearchAPI.io | Best price scaling ($1/1K at 5M tier) |
| **Enterprise / global** | Bright Data or Oxylabs | 195 countries, async, no concurrency limits |
| **Fashion price monitoring** | SerpApi + SearchAPI.io | Best variant/size data + affordable scaling |
| **Multi-engine search** | SerpApi or Bright Data | Most search engine coverage |

---

## 8. Hidden Fees and Gotchas

- **SerpApi:** Only successful searches are billed (good). But at $25/1K searches on the Starter plan, costs add up fast for high-volume use.
- **SearchAPI.io:** The 20%-of-monthly-credits-per-hour rate limit can be restrictive if you need burst capacity.
- **Bright Data:** The $499/mo minimum for volume pricing is a significant commitment. Pay-as-you-go at $1.50/1K is reasonable but still has a learning curve with their async system.
- **Oxylabs:** Enterprise-oriented; expect sales calls and potentially custom contracts.
- **All providers:** Searches that return no results may or may not count toward your quota depending on the provider -- check each provider's policy.
- **Google Shopping specifics:** Some providers charge a shopping search the same as a regular search; others may charge differently. With SerpApi, a Google Shopping search costs one search credit, same as any other search.</result>

Both agents are done. Here's a consolidated summary of the findings:

---

## SERP API Research

### Top Providers Compared

| Provider | Free Tier | Cost per 1K Searches | Best For |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| **SerpApi** | 250/mo | $25 (low vol) → $9 (high vol) | Best data quality, fashion variants |
| **Serper.dev** | 2,500 queries | ~$1-2 | Budget prototyping |
| **SearchAPI.io** | 100 | $4 (low) → $1 (5M tier) | High volume scaling |
| **Bright Data** | Trial | $1.50 PAYG → $1 (2M/mo) | Enterprise, global (195 countries) |
| **Oxylabs** | Trial | ~$1.35 → $0.50 | Cheapest at scale |

### Shopping/Fashion-Specific Winner: **SerpApi**
- Has an **Immersive Product API** returning size/color variants with stock status
- Multi-seller price comparison, sale badges, markdown tracking
- Also covers Amazon, Walmart, eBay for cross-marketplace comparison
- **Limitation:** No SERP API gives you full product details (materials, fit info, full size runs) — that requires direct scraping

---

## Clothing/Shopping Data Scraping

### Approaches Ranked by Cost & Depth

| Approach | Monthly Cost | Data Depth | Maintenance |
|----------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| **SERP APIs** | $25-900 | Shallow-Medium | Low |
| **Pre-built datasets (Bright Data)** | $250-1,500 | Deep | None |
| **DIY scraper** (Python + Playwright) | $300-5K + dev time | Deepest | Very High |
| **Enterprise platforms** (EDITED, Trendalytics) | $2K-8K+ | Deep + analyzed | None |

### Key Findings

- **Bright Data has 77 pre-built fashion datasets** (Zara, H&M, ASOS, luxury brands) starting at $250/100K records — likely the best bang-for-buck for bulk clothing data
- **Direct retailer scraping** gives the richest data (sizes, materials, stock, reviews) but requires proxy infrastructure ($1.50-4/GB for residential proxies) and constant maintenance as sites change
- **Legal landscape** is favorable — hiQ v. LinkedIn established that scraping public data doesn't violate CFAA, but respect robots.txt and don't scrape behind login walls
- **Anti-bot difficulty varies widely** — H&M/Uniqlo are medium difficulty, while Nike/Nordstrom/Shein are hard (Akamai/PerimeterX protection)

### Recommended Starting Strategy

1. **Prototype with Serper.dev** (2,500 free queries) for Google Shopping discovery
2. **Use Bright Data fashion datasets** ($250/mo) for bulk historical clothing data
3. **Build targeted DIY scrapers** only for the 3-5 retailers where you need real-time, deep data
4. Enterprise platforms (EDITED at $30K+/yr) only if making buying/pricing decisions at scale

---

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/johnathanmo/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/4.3.0/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

## Overview

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
3. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
4. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
5. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
6. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill";
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**
- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**
- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**
- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

## After the Design

**Documentation:**
- Write the validated design to `docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Implementation:**
- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

1→# Mirrorless — Full Project Specification
     2→
     3→Mirrorless is a hackathon project (36h, 4+ people in pairs) — an AI-powered smart mirror that provides hyper-personalized clothing recommendations. Users onboard via phone, get their purchase history and style analyzed, then step up to a physical mirror where AI stylist "Mira" gives them outfit recommendations overlaid on their body in real-time. An MCP server exposes user taste data to Poke and other services.
     4→
     5→## Architecture Overview
     6→
     7→```
     8→┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌────────────────┐
     9→│  Phone UI   │────▶│  Next.js on  │────▶│  Python FastAPI │
    10→│ (onboarding │◀────│   Vercel     │◀────│   on Render     │
    11→│  + dashboard)│     │  (frontend)  │     │   (backend)     │
    12→└─────────────┘     └──────┬───────┘     └───────┬────────┘
    13→                           │                      │
    14→                    Socket.io              ┌──────┴──────┐
    15→                           │               │             │
    16→                    ┌──────▼───────┐  Neon Postgres  Claude API
    17→                    │ Mirror Display│     (DB)      (Haiku 4.5)
    18→                    │ (full-screen  │                    │
    19→                    │  Chrome on TV)│              ┌─────┴──────┐
    20→                    └──────────────┘              │  SerpAPI    │
    21→                         │                        │  Deepgram   │
    22→                    MediaPipe                     │  HeyGen     │
    23→                    (pose + hands)                └────────────┘
    24→```
    25→
    26→## Tech Stack
    27→
    28→- **Frontend**: Next.js (mirror display + phone UI + onboarding)
    29→- **Backend**: Python FastAPI
    30→- **Database**: Neon Postgres
    31→- **Real-time**: Socket.io
    32→- **STT**: Deepgram streaming
    33→- **TTS/Avatar**: HeyGen LiveAvatar API
    34→- **Body tracking**: MediaPipe (BlazePose + Hand landmarks)
    35→- **Clothing sourcing**: SerpAPI / Google Shopping API
    36→- **AI**: Claude API (Haiku 4.5 via Anthropic API with OAuth + beta headers)
    37→- **Auth**: Google OAuth (Gmail + Calendar scopes)
    38→- **Deploy**: Vercel (frontend) + Render (Python backend)
    39→
    40→## Core Components
    41→
    42→### 1. Onboarding (Phone → Mobile Web)
    43→
    44→- QR code → Next.js mobile page
    45→- Google OAuth (scopes: Gmail read, Calendar read, profile)
    46→- Collect name, phone number
    47→- Link to Poke signup: https://poke.com/treehacks
    48→- After onboarding, phone becomes session dashboard
    49→
    50→### 2. Data Scraping Pipeline
    51→
    52→**Fast pass (~10-15s, all in parallel)**:
    53→- Recent 5-10 receipt emails from major retailers
    54→- Brand frequency scan from last 100 email subject lines
    55→- Google profile photo for initial style assessment
    56→
    57→**Background deep scrape (async)**:
    58→- Full inbox: all receipts, newsletter subscriptions, shipping notifications
    59→- Calendar events for lifestyle context (gym, travel, meetings)
    60→- Results stream to agent context as they complete
    61→
    62→**MVP cut**: Skip Google Photos analysis
    63→
    64→### 3. AI Agent — Mira
    65→
    66→**Architecture**: Custom event-driven orchestrator (NOT Agents SDK)
    67→- Central event loop receives: voice transcripts, gesture events, pose data, scraping updates
    68→- Batches events and calls Claude API when meaningful input arrives
    69→- Maintains conversation history and user context
    70→
    71→**Voice**: Deepgram streaming STT → Claude → HeyGen LiveAvatar TTS
    72→
    73→**Vision**: Event-triggered snapshots (gesture detected, pose change, user speaks) sent to Claude Vision
    74→
    75→**Tools available to agent**:
    76→- `search_clothing` — Search for clothing items via SerpAPI
    77→- `get_user_profile` — Retrieve user's style profile and preferences
    78→- `get_purchase_history` — Fetch user's purchase history from scraped data
    79→- `analyze_current_outfit` — Analyze user's current outfit from camera snapshot
    80→
    81→**Personality**: Friendly, teasy/jokey, references purchase data ("I see you've been on an Aritzia spree...")
    82→
    83→**Error handling**: Mira acknowledges issues in character + full error logs to console
    84→
    85→### 4. Mirror Display (Full-screen Chrome Web App)
    86→
    87→- Next.js app rendered in full-screen Chrome on external monitor/TV behind two-way mirror
    88→- Webcam feed via `getUserMedia`
    89→- MediaPipe BlazePose for body landmark detection
    90→- 2D clothing overlay: affine transforms to warp clothing images onto body keypoints
    91→- Mira avatar: liquid glass aesthetic, animated, floats around the screen
    92→- Fallback: static side-by-side display if overlay doesn't work
    93→
    94→### 5. Clothing Recommendations
    95→
    96→- **Source**: SerpAPI / Google Shopping API
    97→- **Engine**: Claude (Haiku 4.5) as recommendation brain
    98→- **Flow**: User profile + search results → Claude selects outfit combinations → explains choices with personality
    99→
   100→### 6. Gesture Control (MediaPipe Hands)
   101→
   102→- **Swipe right** → next outfit recommendation
   103→- **Swipe left** → previous outfit
   104→- **Thumbs up** → save/like outfit
   105→- **Thumbs down** → skip/dislike
   106→
   107→### 7. Phone Dashboard
   108→
   109→- **In queue**: Position number + live mirror preview stream
   110→- **During session**: Live view (optional)
   111→- **After session**: Full recap — outfits viewed, favorites, prices, buy links, style insights
   112→
   113→### 8. Queue System
   114→
   115→- Users onboard → enter queue → background scraping runs during wait
   116→- Phone shows queue position + live mirror preview
   117→- Session starts when previous user finishes
   118→
   119→### 9. MCP Server (for Poke Integration)
   120→
   121→- **Format**: Structured JSON + natural language style summary
   122→- **Endpoints**:
   123→  - Read user shopping tastes (brands, price range, style preferences)
   124→  - View previous mirror sessions (outfits viewed, reactions)
   125→  - Webhook: fires on session end with full summary (user ID, outfits, favorites, buy links)
   126→  - Poke texts user with session recap + outfit links
   127→
   128→### 10. Deployment
   129→
   130→- Next.js → Vercel
   131→- Python FastAPI → Render
   132→- Neon Postgres (via MCP tools)
   133→- Mirror laptop connects to deployed backend URLs
   134→
   135→## Team Structure (2 Pairs)
   136→
   137→- **Pair 1 — Mirror Experience**: Overlay rendering, gesture detection, mirror UI, Mira avatar, HeyGen integration
   138→- **Pair 2 — Data Pipeline**: Gmail scraping, agent orchestrator, Claude integration, SerpAPI, MCP server, phone UI
   139→
   140→## Database Schema (Neon Postgres)
   141→
   142→### `users`
   143→| Column | Type | Notes |
   144→|--------|------|-------|
   145→| id | uuid | PK, default gen_random_uuid() |
   146→| name | text | |
   147→| email | text | unique |
   148→| phone | text | |
   149→| google_oauth_token | jsonb | access + refresh tokens |
   150→| poke_id | text | nullable |
   151→| created_at | timestamptz | default now() |
   152→
   153→### `style_profiles`
   154→| Column | Type | Notes |
   155→|--------|------|-------|
   156→| user_id | uuid | FK → users.id |
   157→| brands | text[] | detected brand preferences |
   158→| price_range | jsonb | {min, max, avg} |
   159→| style_tags | text[] | e.g. ["streetwear", "minimalist"] |
   160→| size_info | jsonb | nullable |
   161→| narrative_summary | text | AI-generated style narrative |
   162→
   163→### `purchases`
   164→| Column | Type | Notes |
   165→|--------|------|-------|
   166→| id | uuid | PK |
   167→| user_id | uuid | FK → users.id |
   168→| brand | text | |
   169→| item_name | text | |
   170→| category | text | e.g. "tops", "shoes" |
   171→| price | numeric | |
   172→| date | date | |
   173→| source_email_id | text | Gmail message ID |
   174→
   175→### `sessions`
   176→| Column | Type | Notes |
   177→|--------|------|-------|
   178→| id | uuid | PK |
   179→| user_id | uuid | FK → users.id |
   180→| started_at | timestamptz | |
   181→| ended_at | timestamptz | nullable |
   182→| status | text | "active", "completed", "abandoned" |
   183→
